title: Viennese fingers
subtitle: Piped butter biscuits with ridged surface and chocolate-dipped ends
excerpt: A very soft butter and icing sugar mixture, combined with flour and cornflour, that is pipeable enough to hold a ridged shape through a star nozzle but melts completely on the tongue once baked. The cornflour keeps the biscuit tender by diluting the gluten. Baked pale gold and dipped at both ends in dark chocolate once cool.
sourceNotes: Viennese biscuits are an Austrian style of pressed butter biscuit that became established in British baking from the late 19th century onward. The name refers to the Viennese confectionery tradition rather than a specific original recipe. In Britain they are typically piped into fingers or swirls, sandwiched with jam or buttercream, or dipped in chocolate. The recipe here makes the classic finger version. The cornflour is the functional difference from a standard shortbread: it interrupts the gluten network and produces a more tender, melt-in-the-mouth texture.

content.text: The success of Viennese fingers depends on
how well the butter is beaten

before the flour goes in. Beat the butter and icing sugar for at least 4 minutes until very pale and the mixture has increased visibly in volume. Fold the flour in by hand so you do not knock out the aeration. The mixture should pipe easily through a star nozzle; if it cracks or crumbles, the butter was not beaten enough or the flour was folded too vigorously.

attrs.prepNote: very soft, at warm room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan) and line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat the very soft butter with the sifted icing sugar for at least 4 minutes until very pale and fluffy. Beat in the vanilla. Sift in the plain flour and cornflour and fold in by hand with a spatula until just combined. Transfer the mixture to a piping bag fitted with a large star nozzle.

Pipe 8 cm lengths onto the prepared trays, leaving space for spreading. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until pale gold at the edges but still mostly pale on top. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes (they are fragile while warm), then transfer carefully to a rack to cool completely.

Melt the dark chocolate in a bowl over simmering water or in the microwave in short bursts. Dip each end of the cooled biscuits into the chocolate and lay on a sheet of baking paper until set, about 20 minutes at room temperature.
